$comment: Registered harness baselines (#727 stage-2, Tier 0). Read by baseline-assert.ts, which REFUSES to let a harness print a report when a reading deviates from its row. This is not a promotion gate — gates/*.json ask 'is the model good enough to ship' and are one-sided; these ask 'is the instrument reading correctly' and are TWO-SIDED. A metric 40% above its baseline is as loud as 40% below, because the usual cause is that the number changed meaning.
$why: Two verdicts nearly went out wrong in one arc, both from a harness reporting confidently on a broken instrument. Phase 1 read street token@1 0.348 against a v264 known-good of 0.573 (-39%; a missing map_location plus piece-concatenation welding '5thAve'). Phase 4a measured a resolver rerank while the resolver reached street tier 0/267 — no street shards wired, instrument dark, report read as a finding (VOID; see 2026-07-16-phase4a-rerank-invalid-measurement.md). Every row below is a number one of those incidents would have tripped over.
$registering: A row demands a commit, a command, and a note. A baseline you cannot reproduce from its own row is a rumor, not a baseline. When a number legitimately moves (new fixture, new tokenizer, a real model change) RE-REGISTER with a fresh row and a reason — never widen tolerance to make a deviation quiet. That is silent gate drift wearing a different hat.
$harness_comparability: NEVER register two harnesses' numbers under one id. The `harness` field exists so they can never be compared by accident. Three live JS harnesses, three different systems, all correct: (1) `js-ship-config` — the real BIO argmax decode under production config; (2) `js-summed-bio-segdecode` — oracle-k, whose segment decode scores spans as summed B-/I- log-probs and NEVER reads the learned span head, so its seg@1 is the untrained STAND-IN on whatever model you hand it; (3) `js-span-decode` — the learned span head via trace.spanScores + decodeSegmentationsKBest. The Python probe (scripts/eval_seg_at_1.py) is a fourth: it feeds NONE of the production soft channels and reads token@1 0.4906 where the JS harness reads 0.573 on the same weights (the #718 trap).
$this_rule_has_already_been_violated: On 2026-07-16 the first cut of this file gave oracle-k a `v301` profile mapping its seg@1 reading to `parity.street.seg_at_1@v301` — a LEARNED-span-decode number (0.5768). oracle-k computes the summed-BIO stand-in, which reads 0.449 on those same v301 weights. The profile compared two harnesses through one id, which is the exact thing the paragraph above forbids, written one section above the violation. It was caught by running it, not by reading it. A profile's rows must carry the `harness` the profile is consumed by.
$precision_comparability: PRECISION IS A COMPARABILITY AXIS, not a detail — and it is the easiest one to miss because nothing about it is visible in a number. Every baseline here is measured on an INT8 model: the shipped weights package ships model.onnx int8-quantized (39.4 MB; DynamicQuantizeLinear + MatMulInteger). A FRESH `export_onnx` is FP32 (157 MB = 39.2M params x 4 bytes). Reading an fp32 candidate against these rows attributes the QUANTIZATION delta to whatever you changed. The gate specs cap int8-vs-fp32 at 1.5pp (`int8_vs_fp32_max_delta_pp`), which is LARGER than several cells on the fragment board — so the confound is not theoretical, it would swamp the read. Pipeline: export_onnx -> quantize_onnx -> THEN grade. Quantize on Modal; the dynamo-exported graph trips shape inference in local onnxruntime builds. Walked into on 2026-07-16 while reading the v3.1.0 gate: the read script's md5 check passed happily, because 'differs from the baseline model' is trivially true for a precision change. An md5 difference proves the file changed, not that it is comparable. scratchpad/read-v310-gate.sh now asserts the candidate's precision CLASS matches.
$golden_street_is_a_convention_mismatch: DO NOT read `eval error-analysis --golden v0.1.2` street numbers as capability. Golden v0.1.2 labels street MONOLITHICALLY on ~99.5% of rows (fr.jsonl: 1551 rows, 665 street golds, SEVEN street_prefix golds; us.jsonl: 2956 rows, 2216 street golds, TWO). The model DECOMPOSES — street_prefix + street_prefix_particle + street — so every decomposed parse scores as a BOUNDARY error, and the tell is street_prefix reading 'expected 10, hallucinated 679'. Consequence: golden street recall reads ~16% for a model whose parity street exact-match is ~61%, because parity JOINS the decomposed tags and golden does not. The 2pp DELTAS across two models are still valid (both are measured the same way) — the ABSOLUTE number is not a statement about the parser. Any 'street error is 78% boundary' conclusion drawn from that table is mostly this artifact; use parity or the fragment board, which join. Noticed 2026-07-16 while trying to name the residual behind the v7 street floor.
$config: Every row records the priors that were fed. Production feeds the query-shape emission prior on every path it parses on — `safeClassify` (core/pipeline/runtime-pipeline.ts) and `geocode-core` (#981, which fixed this divergence for the drop-in servers and the geocode CLI). Until 2026-07-16 the eval harness fed none, so it graded a config production does not run: the #718 channel-starvation class one layer up, inside the harness that was supposed to be the correction. Fixed in ffcb8e96; every row below is re-registered against it.
$config_excludes_fst: The FST gazetteer prior is deliberately NOT in this config. Production passes it only from `parse --resolve-db`/$MAILWOMAN_WOF_DB via tryBuildFST; geocode-core and the API/drop-in servers never do. Feeding it unconditionally would match one production path and diverge from the others. Its measured effect on the Paris target class is +0 — 37 of 43 surfaces fire a bias and the score does not move. See 2026-07-16-t1b-gazetteer-feedback-verdict.md.
$registry_pins_reproducibility_not_valid: A baseline registered against a wrong config is self-consistent and will pass forever. This file could not have caught the starved-harness bug above, and it cannot catch the next one. It pins that a number is REPRODUCIBLE, not that it is MEANINGFUL. Validity is a question you answer by reading what production does — not by re-running the harness.
$profiles: A profile maps a harness's OWN metric keys to baseline ids, because the mapping is not derivable. Pass `--assert-baseline <profile>`; omit it for an unregistered candidate. Every row a profile names MUST share the profile's harness — see $this_rule_has_already_been_violated.

v264.description: oracle-k against the shipped model (v6.3.0) on the triaged parity corpus at k=10, production config. Covers all three scored labels — a harness bug that spares street still shows up in house_number. seg/oracle rows are the summed-BIO STAND-IN, not a learned span decode.

v301.description: oracle-k against the span-head probe, production config. NOTE: oracle-k does not read v301's span head — these are the summed-BIO stand-in over v301's emissions. For the LEARNED decode see the `@v301-span` rows, which oracle-k must never be asserted against.

fragment-fr-v264.description: The FR locale fragment board against the shipped model. Board 2 of the two standing boards; `eval parity` is board 1. A change ships when board 1 HOLDS and board 2 MOVES — neither alone is a verdict.

baselines.config: production: postcodeRepair + queryShape + word-consistency heal at WORD_CONSISTENCY_SHIP_DEFAULT; anchor/gazetteer/country lexicons via loadFromWeights. No FST prior (see $config_excludes_fst).
baselines.note: THE canary — 154/267. RE-REGISTERED from 0.573: that number was measured without the query-shape prior production always feeds, and the fix moves exactly one fixture. Phase 1 reported 0.348 against the old value and the -39% was two harness bugs, not a model result. A reading materially off this on v264 means the harness is broken — the model is frozen and published. PROVENANCE: the night-3 postmortem and oracle-k's old docstring both cite 0.584 for this metric; that value is stale under any config and should not be restored.

baselines.metric: street exact-match, segment decode @1 (summed-BIO span stand-in)
baselines.note: The naive re-decode over v264's emissions with NO trained span scorer — span score = summed B-/I- log-probs. Deliberately WORSE than token@1 (0.453 vs 0.577); that gap is what made 'a trained span scorer is necessary' falsifiable. Unmoved by the query-shape fix. Compare ONLY against @v301-summed-bio; the learned decode is a different harness.
Gold street appears in ANY of the top-5 hypotheses decoded from v264's emissions. The rerank ceiling for the untrained stand-in. Unmoved by the query-shape fix.

The night-3 headroom read: the correct reading exists in the top-10 ~75% of the time against a 0.577 top-1. The secondary read the v300/v301 probes gated on ('if seg@1 crosses but oracle@10 is flat, the scorer reshuffled without learning'). Unmoved by the query-shape fix.

RE-REGISTERED from 0.808 — the query-shape prior costs exactly one house_number fixture while winning one street fixture, the only two movements corpus-wide. Registered so a harness bug that happens to spare street still trips something: Phase 1's map_location bug degraded everything, and street was simply the label being watched.

baselines.metric: house_number exact-match, segment decode @1 (summed-BIO span stand-in)
baselines.note: The untrained stand-in is far worse than the token decode here too (0.596 vs 0.801) — the same signal that motivated a trained scorer.

The most sensitive canary in the set: postcode is near-ceiling, so any harness breakage shows here first and unambiguously. A 10% band on 0.986 means anything under 0.887 refuses. Unmoved by the query-shape fix, which is mildly surprising — the prior's known-format hits are postcode-shaped — and is explained by the model already being right on those rows.

baselines.metric: postcode exact-match, segment decode @1 (summed-BIO span stand-in)
baselines.note: Measured live at the registering commit.

baselines.model: v301 (span-head probe, head LR 1e-3; model.onnx md5 add5b344)
baselines.note: 153/267. RE-REGISTERED from 0.5693. The span head costs the BIO head one fixture against shipped (154 -> 153) — still not a measurable loss, and registered so a future span-head change that quietly degrades the token decode is visible.

baselines.metric: street exact-match, segment decode @1 (summed-BIO span stand-in over v301's emissions)
baselines.note: NEW ROW, and the one that exposed the profile bug. oracle-k does NOT read v301's span head, so handing it v301 measures the untrained stand-in over v301's emissions — 0.449, near v264's 0.453, and nothing like the learned decode's 0.5768. The first cut of this file pointed oracle-k's v301 profile at the learned number and would have refused on a healthy run.

baselines.metric: street oracle-recall@5 (summed-BIO stand-in over v301's emissions)
baselines.note: Identical to v264's 0.663 — the stand-in's list is unchanged by the span head, as it must be, since it never reads it. A useful self-check on the harness taxonomy.

baselines.metric: street oracle-recall@10 (summed-BIO stand-in over v301's emissions)
baselines.note: Measured live at the registering commit.
Identical to v264 — the span head is free on house_number.
Identical to v264 — the span head is free on postcode.

baselines.metric: street exact-match, token (BIO argmax) decode @1 — the span harness's own token read
baselines.note: 153/267. Agrees exactly with the oracle-k reading of the same model, which is the cross-harness check that the two agree on what they DO share.

154/267 — UNMOVED by the query-shape fix, but the comparison around it moved. The trained scorer still beats the summed-BIO stand-in by +12.8pp (0.449 -> 0.5768) on v301's own emissions: the arc's actual falsifier, and it holds. But the rank-1 case collapsed. Within-model the margin halves (v301 token@1 152 -> 153 fixtures, so seg@1's edge goes +2 fixtures/+0.75pp to +1 fixture/+0.38pp), and against the SHIPPED model under matched config it is a dead tie: v264 token@1 = 154/267, v301 seg@1 = 154/267. The +0.75pp headline was an artifact of grading the span decode against a starved token decode.

193/267. The LIST is where the span head's value survives: 0.663 (stand-in) -> 0.7228 (learned), unmoved by the query-shape fix. Also the ceiling any parity reranker can reach — Phase 4a's reranker was bounded by this, so even a perfect one lands short of the 0.90 street floor.
207/267. 0.742 (stand-in) -> 0.7753 (learned). The pre-registered secondary read passed: the scorer learned rather than reshuffled.

baselines.metric: share of STREET-FREE fixtures where the decode emits a street anyway
baselines.note: 12/54. THE ROW NO STREET METRIC CAN PRODUCE. Every street harness in the arc filters to fixtures carrying expect.street — seg@1, oracle@5, oracle@10, the Paris board — so a spurious street on a locality-only row ('New York, NY' -> street='new york') is invisible BY CONSTRUCTION. This is the shipped model's own rate, registered as the bar any decode change must not exceed. T1a found the span decode at 0.352 (+58%) on the same rows, which fired the pre-registered kill condition and kept the flag off by default.

baselines.metric: share of STREET-FREE fixtures where the LEARNED span decode emits a street anyway
baselines.note: 19/54 vs the shipped decode's 12/54 — 11 new hallucinations, 4 removed, net +7. STATISTICAL CAVEAT (corrected after first publication): at n=54 the Wilson intervals OVERLAP ([0.132,0.349] vs [0.238,0.485]) and McNemar on the paired discordants gives two-sided p=0.12, so the RATE DIFFERENCE IS NOT ESTABLISHED. What is established is that the 11 new hallucinations are real, individually inspectable failures ('New York, NY' -> street='new york'), and the pre-registered kill condition turns on the failure CLASS existing rather than on its rate. Registered as EVIDENCE, not as a target: this is what keeps the span decode behind an off-by-default flag. The rate question is settled by the T1c fragment board's bare-locality class at n=400 (+/-4.0pp), not by this row. Not a grammar defect (the exported grammar has start/end transitions and an O segment type at index 0, so an all-O parse is representable and cheap) — it is learned miscalibration, the same thin-corpus prior that makes the BIO head read bare toponyms as locality, seen from the opposite side. If a BAN bare-street shard (T2) works, THIS ROW SHOULD FALL. It is the cleanest single number for grading that shard.
GUARD (contextful). With a clean house number the model is excellent — 370/400. This is the number a shard must not buy its fragment gains with.
GUARD (contextful). Identical to street-housenumber: '12 bis' costs nothing. The house number is doing the work, not the affix.

TARGET. Same streets as street-housenumber, minus the leading number: 0.925 -> 0.215. The model reads a designator-led phrase as a LOCALITY, designator included ('Allee Poque' -> locality='Allee Poque'). The house number is not a hint, it is the LICENCE for a street reading.
TARGET. Same mechanism as bare-street; the particle is not the problem, the missing licence is.
TARGET — the polarity class, measured at n=400 instead of the Paris board's 3/15. Worst of the bare classes because the toponym IS a commune, so the wrong default is also the well-evidenced one.

TARGET — the worst cell and the diagnostic one. Digits are present, so the licence IS granted and the model emits street; it then STEALS the digit as a house number and fragments the street around the hole: 'Allee du 11 Novembre' -> street='Allee du' + house_number='11' + street='Novembre'. Mirror image of T1a's span-decode digit-eating; one confusion, both sides.

baselines.metric: share emitting NO street (the assertion is absence) — class bare-locality
baselines.note: GUARD, AND THE ONE TO WATCH. Scores the ABSENCE of a street on 400 bare communes — the class every other street harness drops entirely. It passes for the WRONG REASON: the model calls everything without a house number a locality, and on bare localities that is accidentally correct. PREDICTION: if T2 teaches bare streets, THIS MAY REGRESS, because the class free-rides on the same broken default the shard is there to fix. A fall here alongside a rise in the bare classes means the shard traded one default for another instead of teaching the distinction.

baselines.config: production, minus the FST prior (measured both ways — see the note)
baselines.note: 33/63 on the operator's Paris list — the target class. Identical with the FST gazetteer prior ON and OFF (T1b). n=63 is small: one fixture is 1.6pp, so treat this as a canary, not a board. Its successor is the BAN-sampled fragment board (T1c), which carries confidence intervals.

baselines.metric: share of fixtures where ANY k-best hypothesis reached street-level resolution
baselines.note: THE Phase-4a catcher, registered for what it is: with ban/street-centroids-fr.db wired and loaded, 314 hypothesis geocodes produce street 1, admin 314 — an evidence rate of 1.6%. A reading of 0.000 means the street shards did not load and the resolver is dark; that is the exact state Phase 4a's VOID measurement was taken in (street tier 0/267). Absolute tolerance, not relative: at a count of 1 the relative band is meaningless. Note the deeper finding this row encodes — 1.6% is nearly as blind as 0%, because a bare fragment has no house number for the address-point tier and no locality to scope a street lookup. The resolver cannot arbitrate the class the arc targets.

baselines.metric: share of NL fixtures where ANY k-best hypothesis reached street-level resolution
baselines.command: mailwoman eval oa-resolver --resolve-db <admin + postcode + street shards>
baselines.note: NL and DE are the two locales with enough street evidence to be canaries rather than knife-edges — everywhere else the rate is 0.00-0.03. If NL reads 0.00, the street shards are not loaded and no rerank result from this run is admissible.

baselines.metric: share of DE fixtures where ANY k-best hypothesis reached street-level resolution
baselines.command: mailwoman eval oa-resolver --resolve-db <admin + postcode + street shards>
baselines.note: See the NL row. US reads 0.01, FR 0.03, ZZ/NZ/AU/NO/PT/PL/RO all 0.00 — those are too sparse to distinguish 'dark instrument' from 'sparse data', which is precisely how Phase 4a's first measurement passed unnoticed.

$fragment_board: The fragment board (T1c) is the grading set for the BAN shard (T2). Its first read on the shipped model: with a house number 0.925, without one 0.215 — the house number LICENSES the street reading rather than merely helping it. Targets = bare-street/street-particle/admin-street-homonym/date-name. Guards = street-housenumber/alnum-housenumber (contextful) and bare-locality (hallucination). See 2026-07-16-t1c-fragment-board-verdict.md.
